title: Ph4nt0m 1ntrud3r – PicoCTF
description:
Author: Eric H\
March 28, 2025 · 2 min read
markdown: **Author: Eric H**\ **March 28, 2025 · 2 min read** *** Hey there, fellow cyber adventurers! 👋\ In this writeup, I’ll walk you through how I solved the **“Ph4nt0m 1ntrud3r”** challenge from **picoCTF 2025**. This was a light but fun forensic puzzle involving some basic network analysis and a bit of base64 decoding. *** #### 🔍 Challenge Overview The task provides a `.pcap` file, suggesting a network capture is involved. Naturally, we’ll open it using **Wireshark**, a powerful tool for packet analysis (available at [wireshark.org](https://www.wireshark.org/)). Once opened, we notice several TCP packets, primarily showing traffic between: * **Source**: `192.168.0.2` * **Destination**: `192.168.1.2` * **Protocol**: HTTP (TCP port 80) *** #### 🧪 Packet Analysis Scanning through the packets, something stands out—many contain what appear to be **base64-encoded strings** (you can tell by the `==` padding at the end of each chunk). Using Wireshark’s sorting and filters: * I arranged the packets by **Time (Ascending)** to observe the flow in order. * Then, I inspected the payloads of the last few packets and extracted the following strings: ``` cGljb0NURg== ezF0X3c0cw== bnRfdGg0dA== XzM0c3lfdA== YmhfNHJfZQ== NWU4Yzc4ZA== fQ== ``` *** #### 🧰 Decoding the Payload I took these base64 chunks over to CyberChef (a great decoding tool), and after decoding and stitching them together, I got the full flag: ``` picoCTF{1t_w4snt_th4t_34sy_tbh_4r_e5e8c78d} ``` *** #### ✅ Conclusion This challenge was a great warm-up—it tested basic **network forensics**, **payload inspection**, and simple **base64 decoding**. It’s perfect for beginners looking to get familiar with Wireshark and packet analysis. If you have any questions or thoughts, feel free to drop a comment below. Thanks for reading and happy hacking! ⚔️💻
